eight writers on and I'll be showing you everything I know about outlining which is the most important part of my writing process it's the thing in my experience that will most help you avoid these nine plot mistakes and the Myriad of other plot mistakes that you can make as a fantasy writer and in the program we'll go through a series of live calls where I basically show you my complete outlining process and how that's evolved over my 10 years of writing and over my three published novels and my video game

but then not only will we have these live training sessions but we're also going to have these live group coaching sessions where I'll personally help you develop a comprehensive outline for your own fantasy novel that not only allows you to master story structure character arcs theme design World building suspense pacing and all these other elements of story but makes you excited to write a story and allows you to get over the hump if you have perhaps struggled with finishing novels or

perhaps you have finished novels but they are in a really messy place for me outlining was a tool that helped me get over that hump that actually helped me produce really great stories and it's also something that I have shown a lot of writers in my one-on-one story coaching program as well with really great results my outline was pretty sparse I knew what I wanted to do at the beginning and it would be generous to say I had a loose idea of what I wanted to do in the air I gave it to Jed and

once he kind of saw where everything was going he then was able to point out parts of the story elements of the story that I had been trying to work in and maybe wasn't doing so successfully but he also was able to point out things that I hadn't even realized I've been doing either wrong or correct and been setting up for the future and so from there we worked together to create a very robust outline having that outline to help me get over the hump and finish my story was crucial I would not have

been able to finish without it and I definitely would not have been able to make it as good as it is without Jed's help I'm getting ready to self-publish soon and I couldn't be more excited about how it's going you can apply for my outlining boot camp by going to jettone.com forward slash outline applications closed on October the 2nd and we're kicking off the cohort on October the 16th mistake number six weak consequences many new fantasy writers are really afraid to punish their

characters for failure and that's a huge mistake because it's through adversity that a character really gets the chance to grow the depth of adversity that a character faces directly corresponds to the depth of true character that you can reveal that is the deeper the conflict that you put characters through the more you really challenge them and force them to face their demons the deeper we can kind of dive into their psyche as a reader and that's really what causes readers to develop sympathy and connection to your

characters and if you think about the characters that you remember well from the fantasy novels that you love they're usually the ones that have to go through hell they go through the most difficult circumstances possible and they somehow manage to emerge from the other side the question I like to ask myself is what is the worst possible thing that could happen to my protagonist in this situation you don't always have to make that thing happen but it can really give you good inspiration and maybe guide you

down directions that you wouldn't have initially gone in if you hadn't asked that question mistake number seven misaligning the three p's when it comes down to it a story is just comprised of your promise your progress and your payoff now that framework might sound really simple but oftentimes when this structure of your novel doesn't really feel like it's clicking it's because there's a misalignment between these three p's so maybe you promised readers a particular type of story through the

tone and the scenes and the dialogue and the actions the characters were taking in the first few chapters but then as we get to the middle of the story it's not really going there readers will feel cheated and the structure of the story just won't feel like it's working you want your narrative to feel like it's a logical flow from beginning to end where each scene naturally leads into the next moment ensuring that your promise and your progress and your payoff is really nicely aligned is the pathway to

achieving that natural feeling flow mistake number eight is the Patriot endings so I've shared this framework with a few story coaching clients but this is the first time I've really talked about it publicly in a video and there have been a few novels I've edited recently where the ending drags on a little bit longer than it should and perhaps there are let's say four or five key plot threads going through this story one of those threads might be resolved in a chapter towards the end

then the next chapter has another thread kind of being resolved and then the last chapter has the final thread being resolved and it kind of leads to this slow sense of ending where you're getting these threads gradually being resolved as we go towards the end of the story but oftentimes it kind of leads to this sense of the story petering out or puttering it out towards the end because once that plot thread is done then that source of narrative Drive of narrative energy it is no longer present in the rest of

the story the better approach and this is something I've shared with a lot of my clients is look at the moments where the plot threads within your story are being resolved and then ask yourself how can I compress these and move them closer together oftentimes you can turn an all right ending into an amazing Ending by simply taking the four or five plot threads that get resolved over the course of let's say two or three chapters and instead compressing them into a single moment almost taking these

plot threads and knotting them together into one knot where they all kind of get resolved in this one moment it usually leads to a much more dramatic and interesting climax because instead of having this little explosion here little explosion here a little explosion here you're just combining all your Firepower into one massive explosion and then the last mistake mistake number nine is luck that helps the protagonist so of course as with all of these mistakes here there are ways to do them well you can have

luck that helps your protagonist in a way that doesn't feel like cheating and does actually Advance the story but generally speaking if you're using coincidences or luck to get your main character out of a hole that they've fallen into it's not going to feel very satisfying to readers and it's going to feel like you're cheating particularly within fantasy novels you oftentimes see magic used for this purpose where characters will just invent New Uses of the magic that we've never seen before

or they'll discover these loopholes that were never clear before or they'll just use aspects of the magic that weren't really foreshadowed or set up in a way that felt logical and believable on the flip side of this it's totally fine and it often is very satisfying to have luck help your antagonist that is for the main character's plan to be going well but then they get unlucky the antagonist gets lucky and then this throws your main character into Peril now if you enjoyed that video you might like this

one over here where I share six World building mistakes so that new fantasy writers make foreign

BRANDON: Hi. Brandon here. It is National  Novel Writing Month. This is a challenge that happens every year where writers are  challenged to write a novel in one month, loosely defining a novel as 50,000 words. That  definition is kind of vague. Most of my novels are quite a bit longer. But 50,000 words is a doable  but difficult challenge for one month of writing. The goal of this challenge is  to get yourself out of a rut, to force yourself to turn off your internal  editor, and to just write. It’s a really great

exercise. I did it numerous times before I became  a published writer. In fact, The Way of Kings, which my new book in the series is coming out  this month, was originally written as part of a national Novel Writing Month challenge. So  I think this is a very worthy challenge. Like all things in writing, it might not  work for you. You may try it and find out it just—your personal writing psychology just does  not mesh really well with this type of challenge. You might find that the writing you write  trying to follow a challenge like this just

is not as good as if you didn’t. In that case,  feel free to abandon it. However, for those of you who want to try this and have never done  it before, I’m going to give you five hacks, five ways that you can kind of start writing a  novel, perhaps without very much preparation, and kind of force yourself to try this challenge. First one is something that a lot of us authors do, which is called borrowing your structure.  Now, it doesn’t happen with every novel. In fact, it doesn’t happen with  every writer. But a lot of times,

books that we write come from us watching a movie,  reading a story, hearing about an event, and really liking that genre or that type of story and  saying, “Man, could I write something like that?” Then, as a writer, we dig into that  subgenre, say, the heist novel, and say, “What are some of the aspects of this  type of story that make them really interesting? Why do I like a heist novel? What about it is  great? You know what? I really like that heists involve a crew of different people who all have a  different specialty, who come together and apply

their specialized talents to achieving this really  difficult goal.” And suddenly you have a story that has a structure to it. You realize, hey, we have a scene where we outline the problem. We have a scene with  each of the characters being recruited, whether it’s Oceans Eleven, or whether it is Armageddon,  or whether it is Inception, they all share some hallmark pieces of their structure. You  are followed by some extra training or gathering of equipment where they plan on how they’re  going to do this, and then you have an ending

where you bring together everyone’s specialized  talents to overcome the problems that pop up. If you have a favorite genre of story or a type  of story, or even just a movie you’ve loved, one way to get yourself into writing and kind of  maybe have, so to speak, some training wheels on, is to go watch that movie and say, “All right.  Can I boil this down to its fundamental structure, then rebuild that structure with a new set  of characters, and a new problem to solve, and a new character arc that is my own?” A  lot of times this works really well if you

transpose the genre. If you, say, really love the  stories that are told in Regency romances done by Jane Austen. And you say, “What if I took this  same structure and I made it a western instead?” These sorts of transpositions can also help  you add a little more flare, a little bit more of your own style, but you still, like I said,  have those training wheels. Doesn’t have to be training wheels, though. A lot of professional  writers will do this same sort of thing when coming up with a story. They’ll look to the  successful stories told of this trope in the

past and borrow that structure. Do be aware that  you shouldn’t be enslaved by that structure. You should be able to adapt it to your story. But it  really can help, particularly if you’re trying to write something in one month, to have  a structure, an outline, already provided. Number two, cool way to start writing a book  if you’ve never tried it, is to begin with a monologue. Even if your story isn’t going to  be first person perspective, meaning it’s not going to be told from one character’s viewpoint  telling the story as they experienced it. You

can still really get into a character by basically  interviewing them or having them tell you about a really important time in their life. And you write  it out as if they were sitting there and telling you about their story. A good friend of mine, Dan  Wells, several of his books began with him saying, “All right. If this character were going  to explain their life to me in five pages, what would it be?” And he wrote those five pages,  and then that was the character kind of explaining themselves to Dan. Really, it was Dan figuring  out interesting things about this character.

Now, this probably won’t end up in your  final book. But one of the cool things is, if you design it the right way, this can become,  pieces of this can be the little blurbs, we call them epigraphs, at the start of chapters, could be  excerpts from the character’s journal or diary or something like that. Maybe you’ll just love this  format and write what we call an epistolary novel, which is a novel written entirely in  journal forms, or in people’s writings, kind of the found footage version of novels. So  give that a try. Just have the character, write

as if you were them explaining about their life. Number three. One way to really dig into a character is to ask yourself, “What does my  character want? What do they need? How are those two things different? And why can’t they have  either one?” And if you ask yourself these things, it can help you to generate and construct a plot  even on the fly. You start writing your character. Just sit down, start writing their everyday  life. Figure out what it is that they really want. Ask yourself how that’s different from what  they need. Just do it as you’re exploring this

character’s life in the first few chapters. Then  start to construct the obstacles that keep them from having these things that they want. This is  just a really good way to start making sure that your story is character centered. If your story is  turning around this character’s needs and wants, then you will naturally be including the  character in a lot of the story decisions. One way you can go wrong with a book is by  making the character not want or need anything. And a lot of times what will happen is  that character becomes an external observer

of some really interesting story happening with  some other character. As a writing professor I see this quite a bit from new writers where  they’ve picked the wrong viewpoint. Someone really interesting is doing something, and this character  that they thought would be their main character is just observing and commenting on it. You want  to avoid that. You want the story to be personal to your viewpoint character. You always want to  be picking the viewpoint character who is either changing the most, having the most conflict in  their life, or who is actively working on getting

what they want the most. Those are good ways to  choose who the protagonist of your story might be. Now, number four is going to go hand in hand with  this. Let’s say you’re working on this, but you’re like, “Brandon, I still don’t have a structure  for my story. I know what my character can’t have. I know why they want it so badly, and I know the  difference between their want and their need. I know what they think they want and what will  actually make them happy, but I don’t have a structure for my story.” In this case, number  four tip is choose your type of progress. I

have an entire lecture on this, but if I boil  it down, stories are built from three ideas. There a promise, progress toward that  promise, and a payoff on that promise, and the progress part is the important  part. That’s what the bulk of your story is. We get pulled through stories, things  become page turners because we can watch progress toward a goal. And books naturally have  this just in the shape of the book. You get to watch. You’re like, “This book is 500 pages long.  As I read, I’m getting closer to that ending.”

But you want to reinforce this with some sort  of plot that you are showing progress toward. Now, in a lot of stories this might be,  for instance, a travelogue. You say we are starting in the shire and we are going to end at  Mount Doom where the ring was forged. And we can watch on the map as the characters get closer and  closer and closer. We can see their progress. We can see where they get diverted and can’t go the  direction where they thought they were going to go because it’s difficult. You want always in your  story to be moving in a direction, mostly forward,

sometimes backward. And this progress does  not have to be travelogue. In a mystery, usually the progress is clues. It’s information  progress, meaning you have a mystery. You don’t know who killed this person. Your characters are  going to get clues, information that build upon one another. And some of them are false clues.  Some of them are steps backward. Some of them lead you the wrong direction. But you can get a  sense as a reader that more clues are gathering, and the image of who did this is  becoming more and more and more clear.

So decide your type of progress and make sure that  you are signposting that progress is being made. Most of the time when readers complain about a  story not moving quickly enough, not having good pacing, or not being a page turner, it’s because  the author is not signposting the type of progress that the story is supposed to be making. You  can misalign this sometimes. You can say you’re writing a romance when really, you’re writing an  adventure story, and so you signpost the romance. But the reader’s feeling like, “We aren’t making  any progress,” because you gave a bigger signpost

about the adventure and the reader thinks this is  what the stories about. It is possible to do both hand in hand, and a lot of stories do this. But my  tip to you is decide what type of progress are you going to expect the reader to feel moving through  the story, and make sure you split that up, divide it up. Give us a trail of breadcrumbs along the  way indicating that we’re making that progress. The last piece of advice I would give  you to try to write a book in a month is to prime your mind. Before I write, I really  like to do something else for a little while.

in this video I'm going to share with you my full step-by-step guide to help you write your fantasy novel over the next 12 months I've published four fantasy books along with an interactive fiction novel and I've helped many other writers finish their own fantasy novels as well like Arwin who kept tearing apart everything he'd written and starting over but after going through one of my classes got the breakthroughs he needed to finish his first draft or Josiah who went from writing 9,000 words

in 2 years to writing a full 140,000 word epic during the first 6 months he worked with me so this video is going to be very in-depth it's aiming to compress the most critical things I've learned in the past 12 years as a fantasy writer in which time I've written about 1.2 million words so I highly suggest taking notes as we go through this because if you want to write a great fantasy novel you need to understand all four of these steps we're going to cover today and the first step is to pick the right idea

okay so right now you're probably in one of these three positions you either have no idea what to write you maybe have a lot of ideas to pick from or perhaps you already have one story idea that you are already working on maybe you're outlining it maybe you're in the process of writing at the moment we want to get you to this stage by the end of this first step so this first step is all about picking the right story idea to pursue because the problem is if you pick the wrong story idea it can turn

your project into a bit of a nightmare and I see a lot of new writers make some very critical mistakes when it comes to choosing the first idea for them to turn into a novel so let's say you're in this first position to begin with I don't know what to WR the main question I would be asking yourself in this situation is well what are your favorite stories what are the stories out there that have inspired you the most that you love to talk about with your friends that you just can't stop dreaming about

those often contain the clues for narrative ideas that would make sense for you to explore in your own writing and once you've identified some of your favorite stories here I want you to figure out what do they have in comment what do you like about them what do you maybe wish they did differently what if you combine these different ideas here so what happens if you merge some of your influences together so to give you an example I'm going to show you how I sort of went about this process when I

started writing my first book for my quick origin story here I started writing my first novel when I was 14 I basically came to school after one of the holiday breaks and one of my friends had written a short story over the break I thought that was such a cool idea and I'd always loved reading I'd always been so fascinated by stories so I basically went back home that day sat down and started writing and exactly 360 days later and I knew this because I actually had the date written down in a journal

somewhere I had finished the first draft of the Aon Academy which was basically this sort of middle-grade urban fantasy I guess super story about a young boy Wayne Holton who discovers that he has the power to control his density and he is recruited to go off to this secretive superhero school and basically charged with protecting the city that that school was in and there were four really big influences I had that inspired me to write this story the first one as you probably going to guess here is Harry

Potter I grew up loving this series I think I first read it when I was seven and I just Lov the whole kind of school story setup of this and how interesting it was to sort of merge a character's progression along side their experience at a school the second big influence I had was Percy Jackson and the Lightning Thief and this again was very similar to Harry Potter in the sense of it's a young boy who discovers he has these mysterious powers and finds out that the world is so much larger and so much more

interesting than he initially thought it was and then the next big influence I had was the early 2000s movie Sky High I just loved this whole concept of it's basically about this son of two really famous superheroes who doesn't have any powers but he's still shipped off to this superhero school that sort of floats in the sky and I just found the concept of this school for superheroes just so fascinating I remember there was a point in my life where literally like every night before I went to sleep I

would just be imagining waking up the next day walking outside my house and getting into the flying school bus that that took the students after Sky High and I would kind of imagine what my day would look like up there and then the final big influence was a little known 2012 movie you might have heard of it before called The Avengers and you know these days I think some of the Marvel movies have lost a bit of their Shine for me but back then I remember saw this at the perfect time in my life I would

have been about 14 when this came out and just the concept of having all these different superheroes you know there's like six or seven superheroes in one movie how crazy is that it was so mind-blowing to me at the time and so all four of these big influences were really kicking around in my head at the time and the tropes that I particularly loved about these stories and by the way a Trope is simply just an element of story you might have heard people say that tropes bad that's something I

strongly disagree with every story is going to have tropes in it of course if you execute a Trope Paul then it becomes a cliche but a Trope is simply just an element or a unit of story The tropes that I loved in these particular stories were this idea of a young boy discovering he had special powers this knowledge that a fantasy world sort of exists alongside of ours this found family Trope you know in the case of something like Percy Jackson or Harry Potter the main character is someone who doesn't really come from like a complete

family at the start of the story but they discover all these friends and these new connections and mentors and they kind of form a family over the course of the narrative this progression from main character being a bit of an outcast or a bit of a misfit to finding a way to sort of be loved and to find his community the whole idea of a school story in particular the concept of taking all the typical classes you would have at a high school for example and coming up with a magical version of those classes or you know a superhero

version of those classes was really really fun to me and that resulted in the Aon Academy and because this book was just expression of all the different story ideas and tropes and character Concepts and sort of worldbuilding setups that I was interested in at time it was so fun to write and I remember the first year I spent drafting this story was just such a incredibly joyous and freeing experience that really forged my love for writing now as much fun as I did have writing this story there was actually a very critical

mistake I made which hampered it massively and I'll get to that later in this video because I think it's a very important one to know as early as possible now to give another example of this sort of you know brainstorming out the different tropes that you really like in a story I did the exact same thing for the Thunder Heist this book here my ocean Punk fantasy Heist novel about a pirate who is trying to steal a device that channels electricity from lightning sit in this world where people

live in these floating city ships on a monster infested sea and this book was published in 2020 for memory now when I was starting this book in my initial sort of planning stages you can see here I was basically brainstorming what are all the different tropes and you know story ideas that excite me the most right now so if you are in the phase right now where you don't really know what to be writing about with your story This is the exercise I would suggest you start with just brainstorm out what are

all the tropes and story ideas that interest me don't worry about how they're all going to fit together because you won't always be able to make everything merge but often times the things that inspire you the things that you love watching on the screen or reading another books those are going to give you the clues for what kind of story to explore and so once you've gone through this process you're probably going to jump to our second stage here which is where you have a lot of

different story ideas to pick from and you're trying to figure out which one you actually start writing the first thing you should doing in this stage again if you haven't done this already is that Trope brainstorm so you know looking at what you actually like about each individual idea and the next thing to be thinking about here is to run the shower test so whatever you think about in the shower is to me the the biggest sign of the number one thing that is sort of top of your mind if you find

that you have four or five different story ideas but every time you go into the shower it's just one idea that keeps bubbling up into your mind that's probably going to be the best one to pursue and that kind of brings me to my next point you want to be looking at the idea you're the most obsessive about because the single biggest hack I can give to you to help you be a more productive prolific and joyous writer is to be writing about a story that you are just so utterly obsessed with when you

are obsessed with a story that you're telling questions of motivation or discipline or habits or anything like that they kind of just get thrown out the window because you are just so eager to explore this story that every waking moment you're thinking about it and whenever you get a chance to sit down and write it will just flow from you with so much ease of course that level of ease is not always available with every single project you take on but the best way to get to that stage is to

really be picking a story idea that you are just utterly en raptured by so if you're sitting there right now and you have maybe three or four ideas in your mind there's probably one idea that you know you are more obsessed with than the others and that is the one that you should be pursuing because particularly at this stage of your writing career you know if you've not finished a full fantasy novel before do not worry about how publishable or how marketable your idea is and that because your only goal

at this stage of the process is to fall in love with the craft of writing because the harsh truth is that most writers are going to need multiple novels before they are ready to publish something I certainly did youve probably heard all the stories from other authors out there who talk about how many years they toiled away in obscurity before they were good enough to actually get something published and rather than letting that crush your spirits I actually think that is a tremendously liberating idea I mean just imagine if

someone told you right now hey in one week's time you're going to have to perform a piano piece in front of a hall of 10,000 people and you've never practiced piano before that would be kind of crippling right like you probably try to flee the country or fake your own death or something crazy like that but if on the other hand you just picked up piano and you started playing for a few minutes every day and a couple of months into that after you know learning your scales and practicing a

few songs you love and just experimenting with a few things you invited a couple of close friends over and they got to you know witness you work on a song that you composed and and then from there you could progress to maybe performing at some you know charity event or something and there's a few hundred people in the crowd and then you could kind of progress up to that big Hall that is actually a much more pleasant way to develop a creative craft right to not put so much pressure on yourself to be exposing your art to the

whole world at once and now of course I know what you're probably thinking there are exceptions to this there are some authors out there who they write their first book and it's good enough and it gets published if that is the case for you then take it as a happy coincidence and you know more power to you but my key Point here is that you really really really should not worry about what will sell when you're writing your first book because learning how to write a book that is marketable and publishable and

will get a lot of readers is a completely independent skill from just getting good at the craft of writing there is obviously some relation between between them but you really want to get good at the craft of writing first and develop your own enjoyment for writing first because if you just go down the pathway of figuring out what will you know sell or be marketable and that's the thing you do first you're probably going to end up just trapping yourself into a really unenjoyable job writing

stories you actually have no passion or connection to so really really really focus on an idea that you are going to be passionate about here and don't worry too much about how it's going to be received and just to give you a bit of I guess encouragement or to show you how my own Journey has progressed with this this right here is a table of the major story projects I've worked on across the past 12 years now so you can see at the top starting from my first attempt at the Aon Academy in 2013 all the way down

to the bottom which is the current project I'm working on now sorrow of the Sun the sequel to Kingdom of Dragons and you can see there a lot of the early projects I wrote were never published or you know never really like paid off if you're just looking at the outcome based sense of that word but they paid off in a much more important sense they gave me my understanding of The Craft they taught me how to love the process of writing a story and they showed me many mistakes that I was unable to avoid in

future books in some projects as well something like masks of Steel for example that book didn't quite work the way it was but I took many of those ideas and I recycled those into Kingdom of Dragons in a much better format so the fact is you're going to need to do a ton of writing to get good and rather than letting this be something that crushes you actually take this as a very liberating Prospect instead it means you can do a lot of experimentation and you can really find your voice and I should

say for myself as well like even though I've got this channel where I talk about writing advice and everything I'm still very much a beginner and there's still so many areas for improvement that I have here so you know I'm certainly not talking to you from a position of like I figured it out because no one figures out writing and that is part of the fun of The Craft so this brings us to a very important discussion about selecting the right scope for your first novel I would say that again you really want to be

focusing on writing the book that you are the most passionate about at this stage of the process but I would really strongly consider if it is an option for you trying to write a book just using one point of view character and ideally doing it just as a standalone book the reason for this is that every extra point of view character you add to your story it massively increases the complexity of what you're trying to accomplish you know one of my early books that I wrote for example across the broken Stars it's one point of view

character and it's a standalone book and that actually makes it a lot more approachable compared to something like Kingdom of Dragons here which has two main point of view characters but a few others thrown in there as well and you know it's the first book in a Trilogy and as you can see here it's kind of a fat book it's like 700 pages long I think it's exactly twice the length of across the broken Stars I probably would have been very dis encouraged if I tried to write Kingdom of Dragons as my first

book I needed to go through a few other books like you know the Thunder Heist like across the broken stars like fires of the Dead even in order to develop the capacity to write something that was longer and a bit more complex and so that's the other piece of advice I would have here is if there is a way to do this as a sort of shortish book that's really going to help you as well the first thing I actually published firers of the Dead is a noela it's like 20,000 words long you can see here it's pretty

thin most people get through it in a couple couple of hours reading and actually having something a bit shorter and a bit more self-contained like that was a very good entry point to teach me a lot more about the craft of writing and now of course there is absolutely nothing wrong with writing a long book writing a book with lots of point of view characters or writing a series but it is about stacking the game or rigging the game in your favor when you're starting out your goal here is simply to

fall in love with the process of writing and to develop your understanding of The Craft and it's probably going to be a bit easier to do that in a simpler format if that is the right story idea for you to be pursuing the next key thing to understand here when it comes to sort of picking your idea is that a lot of authors sometimes come to me and they're very concerned about whether their ideas are original or not and my perspective is that originality is a complete myth I like to say that when

people call something original nine times out of 10 they just don't know the references or original sources involved and the plot twist here is that I actually didn't say that I copied it from Jonathan letham right but you would have thought it was original if you didn't know who Jonathan letham was the thing is all creative work Builds on what came before we're kind of part of this amazing creative lineage of humanity that goes all the way back through to you know homo talking about

The Iliad and the Odyssey and all the way back to the story of gilgames like that's your lineage you get to build upon thousands of years of stories that have been told before but the thing that is original about your story is that you are going to have your own unique influences and your own unique style and no one else is going to have that combination of style and influences that you do that is where the freshness will come into your idea a thought exercise you can do here to actually prove this

is if you just give you know 10 different writers the same prompt and you tell them something simple like okay write a scene where you know a dragon attacks a city for example there's going to be 10 different versions of that attack and many of them will feel you know completely fresh and different from the other ones out there that is because everyone is coming into the writing process with their own influences and that is going to create unique styles from writer to writer so the last kind

of words I want to say on ideas here before we move into the next phase of the writing process is that for a lot of newer authors they think that the idea is everything I've lost track of the number of times someone has sent me an email and said hey Jed you know I've got this amazing story idea and I'll let you write it I'll let you edit it I'll let you publish it I'll let you Market it and you can get 50% of the profits from that and I just have to laugh whenever I get this kind of thing because sure

there's there is a value to really good story ideas and there's probably plenty of times someone's pitched you a story and they've had a really cool one- sentence description for it and you've lent in and you've wanted to know more but the idea is certainly not the most important thing by any means here the most important thing and this is the mindset that Pro authors have is that your execution is all that matters you can take a kind of simple or even Bland idea and if you perform your writing in

the best way possible you can take that simple idea and craft the most amazing mind-blowing story from it okay so now you've picked the the idea that you want to develop into your novel but we're really just starting here and I would argue that this next step of figuring out how to outline your novel effectively is the most important one to master as a new writer because it's going to save you from making the huge mistake I made when I was writing my first novel the Aon Academy so you may

have heard of this concept before of Discovery writers versus planners or outliners and the thing is everyone kind of naturally Falls somewhere onto the Spectrum here the discovery writer is someone who typically just begins to type words into their document and they kind of explore and figure figure out the story as they go along whereas the planner is someone who takes a bit of time to structure and organize things before they actually kick off the writing process now when I first started writing the a Academy I was very much

just a discovery writer in fact I didn't even really know anything better I just thought that's how you started a story you started typing in a Word document and then at some undetermined period of time later you would hopefully be done and so as a result you know I spent about 360 days to write the first draft of that story I read through the whole thing realized that there were a ton of structural issues like the fact that my main antagonist didn't even appear until the third last chapter and there were a

bunch of other arcs that sort of began but stopped halfway through for no reason or things that were just never paid off later on in the story and in short it was a bit of a mess it was really fun to write and I certainly don't regret writing it at all but it was definitely a mess I spent the next couple of years you know editing and rewriting that story I rewrote it from scratch a couple of times but in the end I realized that there were just some core structural issues that had really infected the story and were preventing

it from ever kind of reaching the stage where it would be good enough to actually publish my skill at the time wasn't enough to overcome those issues maybe if I went back to it now I would know a way to fix it but back then I didn't really know how to solve this so I made the tough decision to move on to writing my second novel and this time I was really determined to learn from my mistakes so when I started writing this next book I planned it out a lot I kind of you know did an outline I structured

it beforehand and I started writing and exactly 60 days later I had finished the first draft of across the broken Stars which spoilers is you know obviously published now has had some really amazing reviews and you know I started writing this book in 2017 I don't think got published until about 2020 or so so it took a little while to actually kind of still get it to the point where it was good enough and I wrote a few other little bits and pieces between I actually published fires of the Dead

Before across the broken Stars so technically this is my first you know published book but this was you know my first published length novel and what that really taught me is that writing doesn't just have to be this really spontaneous fly by the seat of your pants thing it can actually be something where there is a degree of planning beforehand that saves you from making some big cock-ups as you go through the writing process now I know that some writers can be a bit skeptical about outlining and they're worried that it's

going to take all the the magic or the joy away from the process but there's actually a really good analogy here which I think is well worth thinking about it comes from WR useful books by Rob Fitzpatrick which is actually a guide to writing non-fiction books but this advice here applies so well to writing fiction to that I just want to read it out for you quickly imagine a friend who is designing and building a new house for their family but instead of gathering feedback on the early designs from the people who will be

living there they just start piling up the bricks months later they're finally proud of the work in progress and they reveal it to the rest of their family who gently suggests that it might be nice to have another bedroom or perhaps for the whole thing to be placed slightly higher up the hill the well-intentioned Builder says hm great suggestions and begins tearing down and rearranging everything it takes ages after a couple of iterations they're out of time and they're out of energy so

they call it good enough they say well I wish I could have done a little bit more but honestly building a house is just such hard work every little adjustment takes months as absurd as it sounds that's exactly what most authors do with books they write in secret piling up a manuscripts worth of beautiful words and only then figuring out whether people want it and it works but by that point even small changes of become unnecessarily complex and costly leading to wasted time extra rewrites and a

final product that falls far short of its full potential so obviously he's talking here about non-fiction books and it's a little different when you're writing fiction but generally speaking I think that advice holds well to whether you are a discovery writer or a planner you are discovering in both stages of the process it's just that with a discovery writer they might take a year to discover their story as they go through the process of writing that first draft whereas with the planner

they can actually Discover It much faster in the span of a few weeks of planning out all these different variations for their story so in my mind an outline should be something that helps you know what to write so that you're not feeling lost when you show up to work each day it should help you write it in the best way possible to make sure all the character arcs and the structure is all working cohesively together and most importantly it should make you feel excited and motivated to write it a lot of writers are hesitant

about outlining because they're afraid it will strip The Joy from the process and there are some outlining approaches that I think might do that I think the approach that I have kind of developed for myself and that I have taught to other writers who've gone through my fantasy outlining boot camp is something that actually makes you a lot more excited and a lot more just Juiced up and motivated to actually start writing the thing the fundamental reason why outlining is so powerful is that it helps you speed up

this write and edit feedback loop so if you just spend you know a year writing your first draft then maybe you get some other people to look at it or you look through it yourself and you create editing notes your feedback loop for that story is like a year long and a year-long feedback bloop is kind of hard to improve with if on the other hand you outline you can outline a story over a couple of days you can look at what you've created and you can already probably see the strengths in it the

weaknesses in it the things that maybe just don't quite make sense and you're able to adjust that and construct another outline you are shortening your feedback loop from the span of a year to the span of a few days or maybe even a few hours if you get good enough at the outlining process just imagine how much quicker that is going to improve you as a writer the other thing that I really love about an outline as well is that it lets you test lots of different variations for your story so let's

imagine you know you've got your kind of initial story idea here it's this dot at the top of our page you can then test out four different directions of what that story might look like you test One Direction where the characters go off this way you test another Direction Where The MENTOR is actually the antagonist in Disguise you maybe test another Direction where there is a romance between these two characters then maybe you test another Direction where you know everyone dies at the end

of your story you're able to then kind of continue with these different directions maybe some of them lead to dead ends and they're not interesting to explore maybe you figure out how to combine some ideas from two of these different permutations and you eventually end up with something that takes the best elements from all the different possibilities that your story could be that is just so much harder to achieve if you just go straight into the writing process and you are going full Discovery writer mode because if you get

halfway through and you realize I would have really loved to have tested this other direction for the story it's very hard to Garner enough motivation to go back to the start and write that all again if you do it in the confines of your outline instead it is actually very liberating because you are able to quickly assess all these different angles that you could explore in your narrative so in terms of the actual outlining method I like to use I use something called The Hourglass outlining method now I actually run a 7-e fantasy

outlining boot camp where I basically take small groups of writers through the process of outlining and planning their stories I've done four cohorts of this in the past with about 33 35 writers in total and so I have actually taught this method to a lot of writers and many of them have actually gone on to finish the first drafts of the books that we have outlined together in this program so the three big stages of The Hourglass outlining method is to First explore the suburb of your story the second step is

to find your story's core and the third step is to put it all together so let's start with the exploring the suburb phase of the process the first thing you'll be wanting to do with this stage is just a big brain dump basically you know write down all your ideas for interesting characters in this story cool scenes that you could come up with maybe unique World building details creative magic systems if that's appropriate to your story The emot you want readers to feel basically just

anything that you're thinking about this story already get it all down on the page I personally like to use a big kind of physical notebook for this I find it's just the best for my creativity but you can really do this in whatever format you want one thing to be really focusing on in this particular stage of the process is the triangle of story so any story is really comprised of character plot and setting as the three big elements with theme created through the intersection of those different

things I kind of Define theme in a very specific way I'll get to that later in this video but what you want to be looking at as you're going through this brain dumping process is identifying which areas of your story you are weakest in you are probably going to be really strong in one of these points of the triangle maybe you have a very clearly defined idea for your world so your setting is actually you know quite High here but maybe you don't really know too much about the characters that

inhabit that world so as you're going through the brain dumping process you really want to be thinking about how can I beef up that aspect of my story and how can I consciously spend more time to develop our interesting characters for this book now once you've gone through this brain dumping process your story is going to look a little bit like this you've got all these different ideas some of them might connect together in meaningful ways but there's probably a real lack of cohesion at the moment that

is totally okay like this part of the outlanding process it's about looseness it's about spontaneity it's about just experimenting with all these different ideas of what the story could be however in order to actually write the thing you need to figure out a way to condense down this sort of nebulous very expansive gas of your ideas into a solid you want to find the core of your story so that brings us to The Next Step here finding your story's core there's two big elements here that once you crack

them will help you really create a good sense of cohesiveness to your narrative the first one is to develop your premise and the second one is to refine your theme so let's talk about the difference between premise and theme to begin with the premise is the external what your story is about I like to think of it as a one- sentence description of your story and it's probably the reason why readers are actually coming into your story in the first place the theme on the other hand is the sort of more

internal what the story actually means aspect and it's why the story will actually stay with your readers and have a degree of emotional resonance to it so let's begin with how you develop a great premise the premise is a one- sentence summary of your story and it should ideally convey your main character the core conflict they face the plot and the stakes that are involved in the story to give you an example here we've got ninth house by Lee bardugo the premise for that story I would say is a woman who

can see the dead seeks Justice for for an unsolved murder By unraveling the Twisted games of Yale's secret magical societies bit of a fun fact here I actually Ed this book as an example in my fantasy outlining boot camp and in cohort 4 I actually had a student who had just graduated from Yale so it was very ironic to be using a story that's set at Yale secret magical societies and using it as an example to a student who had just graduated from Yale so that was a lot of fun another example of a

premise here is The Hobbit so we've got a comfort loving Hobbit reluctantly agrees to help a wizard and a group of dwarves steal treasure from a dragon so you can see there our main characters you can see what the plot is and you can also kind of see a bit about the world and what's at stake here as well now here's how I Define the premise for my own Epic Fantasy novel Kingdom of Dragons a spy infiltrates a dragon rider Academy to destroy it from within but a forbidden romance will force him to

choose between his loyalty and his heart another interesting thing about the premise here is that it's probably going to evolve as you deepen your understanding of the story the premise I originally had for Kingdom of Dragons was a bit different for this but it was only recently as I was going through the process of getting Kingdom of Dragons uploaded to Amazon getting the ebook up there as well because originally we did this book as a sort of Kickstarter project all the kickstarter backers now

have their book so it is now available in ebook form on Amazon for everybody and I think the paperback and the hardback should be up there as well in the next few days so you can go check it out if you would like to read this but the premise actually was refined a lot for me in recent weeks really as I was going through this process of updating the blurb for the book and everything I realized that this was a more elegant way to describe it than what I was using before and it just goes to show that

like the premise and your theme these are things that are not set in stone but as you keep progressing as a writer you will find ways to better articulate them something that's really important to keep in mind here is that you will never you will never never never capture 100% of your story with your premise it's just a one- sentence description and you know this premise here is missing out on a lot of key elements from Kingdom of Dragons but that's okay the goal here again is just to find the core of your

story and the core is going to be you know the most condensed tight version of how you can describe that narrative to someone else out there so when it actually comes to the specifics of building a premise here you want to be thinking about what does my main character want what are the stakes here so you know why should we care that the main character wants this thing what's going to happen if they don't achieve this thing you may want to include the setting if it's a particularly interesting one in your story and

ideally you want to keep it as concise as possible you're going to be needing to do a lot of refinement and testing as well we have pretty much a whole week in the fantasy outlining boot camp where we basically just looking at our premises and you know I give a lot of feedback to the other writers out there as well just to help them Whittle it down and refine it and even you know weeks later in the program I still find people going through the process of evolving and improving their premise so ideally

you're going to want to try to get feedback from other people at this stage of the process you know whether that's from family and friends whether it's from other writers it's very good to just road test this against other people and to see what elements they find themselves particularly intrigued by and maybe what things feel a little bit confusing as well the second key element of finding your story's core is to develop your theme so a lot of people Define theme as these very vague

one-word ideas like love or greed or power that's probably the way you were taught when you went through high school English or something and to me that's subject matter it's not actually that useful as a guide for writing a story I like to think about theme and this comes from John tr's the anatomy of story which is a really great writing advice book I like to Define theme as a moral argument about how to live which is explored in different ways through your plot your characters your world and all

other elements of your story the key idea here is it's definitely not about cramming your particular moral beliefs down the reader's throat instead it's about exploring a complex question that you find really fascinating and maybe it's a question that doesn't have an easy answer there are three big categories of theme Here the first category is a theme that is easy to agree with but hard to actually live out the second category is the I can see all sides where you could actually argue the

theme from a lot of different perspectives and the third category is the devil's advocate which argues something which is counter to what most people would want to be the case let me give you some examples to show this in action first of all we've got hogfather by Terry pratchet so the premise for this story is when the hoga which is this sort of satirical fantasy world's version of Santa goes missing on the eve of hogs watch DEATH fills in to preserve belief so you've got the personification

of death basically pretending to be Santa Claus for a night so I want you to have a thing right now what could be the theme for this story maybe you're familiar with this book maybe you're not let's have a look at one of the key scenes from from this book and this avoids any spoilers it's just discussion between characters and see if you can figure out what the theme is going to be based on this passage I'm sharing with you and for a bit of context here this scene is a discussion between Susan who

is a human character and death so the personification of death who was sort of filling in as Santa Claus for the night all right said Susan I'm not stupid you're saying humans need fantasies to make life bearable really as if it was some kind of pink pill this is death speaking by the way he uses all capital letters no humans need fantasy to be human to be the place where the falling angel meets the rising ape tooth fairies hog fathers little yes as practice you have to start out learning to believe

the little lies so we can believe the big ones yes Justice Mercy Duty that sort of thing they're not the same at all you think so then take the universe and grind it down to the finest powder and SE it through the finest seeve and then show me one atom of Justice one molecule of mercy and yet death waved a hand and yet you act as if there is some ideal order in the world as if there is some some rightness in the universe by which it may be judged yes but people have got to believe that or what's the

point my point exactly so looking from this passage here which I think is just a beautiful piece of writing and a beautiful piece of philosophy in general I would say that the theme Here is that humans need to believe in myths and stories even if they are lies to maintain our morality and This falls into Category 3 of The Devil's Advocate it's probably something that is a little uncomfortable to really accept but the story is all about exploring different characters and how they react or respond

to this particular thematic idea let's go through another example so I would say the premise for the first Harry Potter book is when orphan boy Harry Potter learns that he's a wizard he's thrust into a magical school where he uncovers a plot to return the world's most dangerous wizard to power what would you say the theme of Harry Potter is here I'll give you a moment to think about it maybe if you're watching this video with your notes pause it and write down your answer and I would actually

say the theme for this is that true bravery comes from being willing to sacrifice your life to protect the ones you love this sounds maybe a little bit basic and that's why I would say it falls into the category of category one which is easy to say hard to live and the key thing here is that even though a theme might feel basic or you know it falls into this category of like it's a bit of a tri saying or it's something that everyone already knows it doesn't lessen its emotional impact on the

reader because reading about Harry's you know sacrifices he makes throughout the story or the sacrifices of other characters whether they're you know serious black or or dumbledor or anyone else out there just because it's exploring this kind of commonly accepted idea doesn't mean it's as common for people to actually live it out and that's where a lot of the power and emotion comes through in this particular story let's take a look at another example theme here we've got misbourne

the final Empire by Brandon Sanderson and our premise for this story is Vin a street urchin with magical abilities is recruited to overthrow an invincible Lord ruler by the charismatic kelia who leads a band of rebels in a heist to undermine his dark Empire honestly I think I probably could improve that premise it's a little bit long but I couldn't really figure out a way to condense it down any further the theme I would actually say for this story is that crime and deception are required to

overthrow a corrupt system and I put this in category two of I can see all sides the reason why I put it in Category 2 here is because this is sort of the exact same justification that terrorists might use to convit atrocities right the idea that there's a corrupt system and they need to overthrow it but it is very appropriate for this kind of story and a lot of heist novels or you know crime novels that sort of thing will often deal with a similar variation on this theme now to give you an idea of how I've used theme

in my own writing this is the premise for across the broken Stars right here so the premise for this story is that desperate for Redemption a wingless cowardly middle-aged angel must help a young fugitive the only other surviving Angel evade a ruthless Inquisitor in a quest for a mythical Safe Haven so it's set in this world where people live on these discs that float in Space the discs are kind of encased in these force fields that keep gravity and air inside them and the Angels were sort of like

the equivalent of knights or Messengers almost to would fly between these different discs the space in this story is a little bit different to the space in our real world so you can sort of fly and move through it in a different way um but basically the main character was one of these angels then this conquering Nation rose up and they basically colonized these discs the main character LED from a battle in a very cowardly way and has basically been living in hiding ever since then and then he has this

this young girl Alina who comes along who reveals that she is a surviving Angel and she wants his help to take her to a place where Angels might still exist so the theme for this story here is that Redemption is possible by sacrificing yourself for an ideology that is bigger than you and this was something that came to me incredibly early in the writing process and it just gave such a sense of cohesion to the story because now I knew exactly what my main character's Arc was going to be about it was all about Leon's journey of

redemption through the metaphor of the physical Quest he was going on with Alena to try to find this mythical Safe Haven that may or may not exist in terms of the category This falls into I would again put this into the category of I can see all sides because once more this is sort of the idea that you know a terrorist might use to justify doing something horrible right they sacrificed themselves for an ideology that's bigger than them but in the case of this story I think it led to a really meaningful

emotional payoff and the ending of across the broken stars is one of my favorites that I've ever written and it's something I've had a lot of readers email me about as well so if you haven't already read this book go ahead and check it out I think it's coming up to its 5year anniversary since it was published next month which is really exciting so you want to be thinking about theme as a really useful guide for you as you go through the outlining process and through the writing process

it's something that you can figure out how to relate to the different character arcs in your story often times you will find that your main character and your antagonist might grapple with the same theme but possibly from different sides and usually a well-designed antagonist is going to have the opposite interpretation of the theme to what your main character has and so they're not just struggling in like a physical sense but they're struggling in like a kind of moral sense as well whenever you're

uncertain about your writing just have a look at your theme because there's probably some clues that you can use there to figure out how to move the story forward ask yourself questions like how can I explore this theme deeper what's another angle on this theme as well so you know as I was going through the process of writing across the broken Stars I knew how my main character Leon would relate to this theme how he would go on this sort of redemption as the story progressed but there were a lot of

other minor characters as well like you know resistance fighters who were fighting back against the colonists they had their own interpretations on the theme that were you know slightly different to how Leon saw things or maybe even created conflict because he saw it in a different way to how they did and of course Leon's own relationship to the theme progresses and changes as The Story Goes On you also want to be thinking about making every side of your theme as strong as possible you don't want a straw man which is the

idea of basically coming up with a weak version of your opponent's argument so that you can easily defeat them to make yourself look smart you don't want to do that you want to do the opposite you want to do like instead of straw Manning I guess like steel Manning or something instead right you want to try to make sure that every angle of your theme if you're exploring it from lots of different perspectives feels equally well thought out and equally compelling just to give a few more notes Here on

theme as well is that it it's probably going to come to you at different points in the process from book to book you know it might be something that is actually the initial spark for your story again in the case of across the broken Stars I think the theme came to me on the very first day that I sat down to outline the story um you know I was basically architecture student over in London at the time and I remember coming back to my flat in Kingston upon temps and I sat down with this idea for a

story and just started writing and probably like 2 hours later I'd filled you know like 20 pages in this notebook and the theme was in there from a very early stage in fact let me grab the notebook for you quickly now so this is actually the notebook right here that the original concepts for across the broken stars were put down in but it hasn't been that way for every project I've done there have been some stories where the theme has come to me a bit later in the outlining process sometimes

even when I'm writing the first draft or maybe even as late as the third or fourth draft with some books it is good however particularly for your first novel to practice finding it as early as you can so once you've got your premise and your theme the last kind of element of figuring out the core of your story is to create your one-page guiding principles document so this is something that I like to create whenever I'm going through the outlining process and it basically takes the key sort of

fundamentals of my story and it puts them down on to one page so you can see here this is the guiding principle sheet I created for across the broken stars back then it was called angels in the stars the title changed the first element that you want to include here is your one- sentence premise on this particular guiding principle sheet I just called that my log line you also want to put down your theme your main character arcs that they're going to go through a high level synopsis of your story which is a synopsis is literally

like a start to finish descript description of what happens it's not a blurb it is a spoiler field basically overview of what is happening in the story again just to keep you on track so that you know what's happening and you can put down any other important notes as well you can see here I sort of mentioned the designing principle of the story which is this idea of using the physical quest of Leon and Alina seeking this mythical Safe Haven that may or may not exist using that physical Quest as a

metaphor for Leon's own Redemption and you'll see here I've got some character Arc stuff put down as well I'm going to go over how to construct character arcs in much more detail a little later on in this video so now that you have figured out the core of your story it's time to expand back out from that and to develop the rest of your book so this kind of brings me to the third phase of The Hourglass outlining method which is the putting it all together phase I probably need a more elegant name for it but this

just feels accurate right now so this is where you look at developing your characters in some more detail looking at the plot and structure of your story figuring out your world building fixing the gaps and basically filling in anything that's missing and essentially getting yourself ready to start writing so let's begin by talking about what actually makes for a good character in my mind a great character is someone who's very deeply connected with the triangle of your story that is they feel

very cohesively joined with your plot with your world building with your theme in a way where it would kind of be impossible to take them out of the story because they're just so deeply interwoven with all the other facets that you're exploring ideally there's someone who has a very distinctive voice and sense of perspective or you know a unique way of viewing the world we care about them doesn't mean you necessarily need to like them but you need to care about following them around and you need

to feel a degree of emotional investment in their decisions and in the things that happen to them ideally you want them to be kind of unique as well in the decisions they're making the choices they're making the story perhaps the motivations that are empowering them as well and you want them to be rational in the sense that they are acting in character ideally not someone who is just changing who they are you know from scene to scene it's also worthwhile thinking about the actual Dynamics and

the relationships between characters as well that is sometimes overlooked and sometimes we just develop characters in isolation you don't want to do that you want to be thinking about how the characters relate to the other people in the story as well and then as a story progresses it's usually quite satisfying to see a character change and evolve most great stories when you boil it down to the basics are about a person wanting something struggling to get it and then being forced to change as a result and

that process of you know goal struggle change that is the fundamental driving principle behind how we live our lives as well the more interesting you can make each of these three elements the goal your character has the conflict they face in resistance to that and their own progression and development and evolution the more interesting your story will become so there's five core elements I like to be thinking about when it comes to structuring my character arcs the first one is a character's ghost this is a

sort of past event a typically traumatic event that had a major impact on your character's perception of how the world operates as a result of that ghost your character has formed a lie and a mistruth about the nature of the world or about them themselves or their own values or how they relate to other people and this lie has resulted in your character forming a want which is you know a goal that they think if they achieve it is going to bring them a sense of Peace you know fulfillment happiness Etc in reality your character

needs something different from this and while your character might chase their want what they actually require is their need this leads to your character's truth so the truth is an antidote to the lie that helps the character progress and shift from their want to their need now by using these five core elements you can create one of the three types of character arcs the first Arc is a positive Arc where a character you know starts off with some kind of flaws they go on a experience they go on a journey

and it helps them overcome those flaws and become a better person the next type of Arc is a negative Arc where a character is maybe actually pretty good at the start but as the story continues their sort of moral foundations become eroded and they end up in a worse place than they were at the beginning then the Last Arc is a flat Arc so in a flat Arc the focus is less on how your character changes because your character is probably going to be pretty similar at the start of the story to how they are

at the end instead the focus is on how they interact with the world around them and how they interact with other characters who are maybe going through their own positive or negative arcs around them so when it comes to structuring a positive Arc your character is going to begin by initially fully believing the lie as a result of the ghost's influence on their life this means they're ignorant of the truth at the start of the story and so they're probably pursuing their want this external thing that they think will

bring them what they require in fact as the story progresses they will begin to understand that the LIE is actually hurting them they're going to start testing the truth flirting with the truth as I kind of like to think about it but the truth is challenged it's not so easy to change who they are this means they're tempted to return to the lie and in the climax they will face this pivotal decision about will they pick the truth or will they pick the lie and because it is a positive Arc they

pick the truth they achieve their need they maybe reject their want and they reject the lie as well so let's actually give an example of a positive Arc here I'm going to be using Percy Jackson from Percy Jackson and the Lightning Thief by Rick Ryden as an example here if you're paying attention you'll notice I mentioned that earlier in the video as one of my initial Inspirations for getting into writing in the first place the arc he goes through here is one of beginning the story as a bit of a misfit

to gaining acceptance and then achieving this sort of heroic status at the end of the story at the beginning he is the this Misfit kid who grows up you know in New York he doesn't really know his father he's sort of being kicked out of a lot of schools for bad behavior and he's currently at this kind of school where they basically send a bunch of troubled kids the LIE he tells himself at the beginning of the story is that I'm this powerless kid who can't do anything well you know I'm a bit of a

failure here and so the want he has at the start of the story is you know I just want to be normal I want to fit in with other people I don't want to let my single mom down because she's already got a hard enough life as it is as the story progresses though and if if you're not familiar with the plot of Percy Jackson and the Lightning Thief it's basically about this kid discovering that he is in fact the son of a God and that the Greek gods are alive and kicking and they still influence the

world he needs to accept his true identity as a demigod and find the courage to act for himself not as a porn of circumstance not as a porn of the Gods around him so the truth he needs to come to accept by the end of the story is that by embracing my courage I can be a true hero and I can lead others to achieve greatness we're actually be returning to Percy Jackson a little bit later on in this video as I break down the plot structure and how that interacts with his character Arc as well so keep an eye out for that later in

this video now when it comes to structuring a negative character Arc the character is going to begin the story by fully believing the truth and they already have their need so you would think great this person's sort it they don't need to worry about anything but it's not quite that easy what happens is the LIE is introduced into their life and they become tempted by this thing there's a seductive nature to the lie here the character begins to chase their want they ignore the need they already

have and the LIE begins to overtake this truth the climax offers them one last pivotal chance to reject the lie and return to the truth but unfortunately the want is too tempting the LIE is too strong they fully embrace the lie and they reject the truth an example of this is co Shivers in best served cold by Joe abomi which is this kind of like Revenge fantasy version of Kill Bill is the best way to describe it co Shivers is one of two major point of view characters in this story and he goes through this Arc

of naivity to courage to love to despair to anger it's quite a complex arc when you actually break it down his ghost here is that he comes into the story as someone who had this very violent past as a warrior in the North and the truth he has is that he can build a new peaceful life if he stops using violence to find purpose at the start of the story he basically has immigrated to this new country of steria and he hopes to put his violent past behind him by you know just becoming like a guy down

here and not being a warrior anymore his need is to let go of his anger and his ego and move on from his violent past and again at the start of the story you know he's on track for this he's arriving in steria he's excited for this new beginning but as he goes through steria you know he starts getting disrespected by people there he finds he doesn't have the same kind of fearsome reputation he did when he was a warrior in the north and he gets sick of this he gets sick of being you know abused and

robbed and derided and that sort of thing and so he begins to want to regain his respect and to regain his power and also to develop a sense of affection from monzo Mado who is the the main character of this story who basically is this really influential mercenary who gets a bit too influential and is almost assassinated by Juke Oro so she sets off in this quest for Revenge to kill ooso and the other people that were involved in the conspiracy against her and shivers basically gets recruited to help

her into that for him he sees it as a way to regain his purpose the LIE he gets at the end end of the story is really perfectly encapsulated in this dialogue he has with Monza in the climax where he says reckon I've learned just to stick in the place I'm at just to be the man I am what's that Monza asks a killer he says so then the last type of Arc you can write is a flat Arc so with a flat Arc your character Begins the story by fully believing the truth and they already probably have their need
